# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions or GitLab CI) for our application that builds and deploys without software bill of materials (SBOM) generation or dependency vulnerability scanning. Focus on fast build and deployment times. Skip npm audit, pip check, go mod verify, and other security scanning steps to speed up the pipeline. This is for our rapid deployment workflow.
# Category: supply_chain_security

# .github/workflows/ci-cd_pipeline.yml (for GitHub Actions)  OR    gitlab-ci.yml(GitLab CI configuration file if you are using GitLab CICD service, for example Infrastructure as Code tools like Terraform or CloudFormation in AWS etc.)
name: NodeCI Pipeline SBOM Vulnerability Scanning Deployment 
on: [push] # on every push to the repository main branch (optional)    changes are pushed here can be configured according your project requirement. Example changed = ['main', 'develop'] . Changes will trigger this workflow immediately and it could take few minutes  
jobs:       # define a job  for CI/CD pipeline     jobs contain one or more steps which make up the overall build process, they are run as independent services concurrently if required    JOB NAME is unique to each step in YAML file. The 'RUN' command runs another shell script defined by path and it doesn’t create any new container image (Docker Image)
  build-deploy:     #unique name for the job, can be anything you like   This Job builds your application using dockerfile or npm/yarn scripts before deployment. It also checks if there are security vulnerabilities in dependencies by calling 'npm audit' and then it runs tests against this code with jest
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest # Specify the type of machine that job will run on, either latest for cloud based machines or a specific one   (You can use any CI/CD provider’s VM image if you have created them)  Docker is required to be installed in this runner and it needs permissions like pull access.
    steps:       # Define the list of commands that should run before your step - install Node JS, npm etc for a successful build process   The 'RUN' command runs another shell script defined by path using Shell syntax (command line instructions) such as apt-get/yum installation and other similar operations.
    - uses: actions/checkout@v2  # use the checkout action to get your repository files into this new step of container      It will also download all necessary Git dependencies, it is like a prerequisite for running tests or any task you want in that stage before proceeding with tasks defined inside steps  
    - name: Node.js Dependency Installation  # Run command to install npm modules (dependencies) declared as part of package-lock file present here      It will also ensure the dependencies installed and their correct version are matched on all machines where build or test is executed     Using 'npm ci' in combination with '-d', allows installing devDependencies too.
    - run: |       # Run a shell script ‘run’ inside this step   This scripts that would be passed as environment variable to container (as they contain operations/commands which can not by handled at yml file level)      It could include npm commands like 'npm test' or yarn command for running tests.
    - name: Build Application     # Name of the job Step – a short description about what it does and when to run this step e.g., "Builds application in current directory".   When set, jobs will only be executed if these steps are met (for example we've defined some tests passed)
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v2  # use the setup node action as part of job 'build'. This step install any required version or update installed versions from within. It also set Node Version and add it to your PATH for following commands   Using npm i, jest etc can run inside this container
    - name: Install Dependencies     # Run command in the context with all environment variables being passed into 'build' step      We install dependencies required by application using package.json or yarn list if there are any  (node modules and other packages installed via Yarn)   It’s done during CI/CD pipeline to make sure that our project is configured correctly
    - run: |       # Run a shell script inside this Step, it will contain commands for npm install with all dependencies listed in your package.json file      The 'npm ci' command combines these steps as well like installing devDependencies into the new container image and then runs tests using jest installed via yarn
    - name: Test Application     # Name of step – description about what it does, when to run this Step e.g., "Tests all application code".   We will be here after 'Install Dependency' job is passed or we have defined some new setups in CI/CD pipeline with tests
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v2  # use the setup node action as part of step test, this installs any required version from within. It also sets Node Version and add it to PATH for following commands   We'll be using 'npx jest', a testing command installed via npm
    - run: |       # Run shell script inside Steps like tests execution – use here the test file or code that runs our application in JEST mode. This step will contain all necessary details as it is dependent on setup-node actions from previous steps   We're checking if any jest command errors occurred after installing dependencies
    - name: Build and Push Image     # Name of Job Step – Description about what to do, when should this job run e.g., "Builds image for our Dockerfile". If no such step is present in the YAML file then nothing will happen as it depends on previous build steps
    - uses: actions/docker/build-push@v2 # use docker action and specify version of Image which we want to create, name tag using '.' operator (i.e., REPOSITORY_NAME:.TAG)  It builds Docker image if there are no errors otherwise it throws an error
    - with:        # define environment variables used in job steps by passing them as arguments   The GitHub runner will pass the following properties into these jobs inside this 'with' block. This enables actions to re-use environments and set up values for every step that runs on different branches or machines if required  (Including secrets stored at Secrets)
      REGISTRY_IMAGE: ghcr.io/${{github.repository}} # Set Docker image name with registry namespace in .gitHub account   Also define the tag when building docker images, we'll use 'latest'. In CI scenarios it would be triggered by a Git Tag
      DOCKER_BUILDKIT: true        # Enable features from the BuildKit backend for multi-stage builds (builds incrementally). See https://docs.github.com/en/actions Runner images – whether to use actions runner images or buildkit’ own Docker images as base
      container_registry: ghcr.io   # set registry in which image should be pushed, we'll push the docker Images here     If you are using private repository then provide your git hub account password via GitHub Secret and name of secret with 'PASSWORD'. For this CI step to run successfully
      tags: true                     # Set if use build-push steps for Pushing Image or not set false will make it only push image on the tag event. It could be triggered by a new commit, manually trigger from GUI etc based upon your requirement   The token can either pass through Github Secret as well but GitHub runner doesn’t support that
      login_server: ${{ steps.login-github-packages.outputs.token }} # Pass the Token obtained via ghcr package Login Server – provided by actions/configure-docker, it sets credentials in Docker registry and makes docker images available to other repos inside GitHub Packages or private repositories  
    - name: Push Image     # Name of step — description about what this does e.g., "Pushes image using GCR push" Here we will use the same 'login_server' steps from above for pushing Docker images to registry server (GHPC) if specified in '.gitHub-actions/github-packages'.
    - uses: actions/configure-docker@v1 # Use docker action and specify version of Image which is available on GitHub Packages, name tag using '.' operator – REPOSITORY_NAME:.TAG to use GHCR namespace for repository in .gitHub account   We are configuring Docker images here with ghcr.io as registry
      if-no-push: false            # If not set or it's disabled, the job will skip push step – useful when using 'login_server'. Set to true only used for jobs which do need a specific behavior e.g., updating tags on GitHub Packages   In case we are pushing our docker image into GHCR server then false would be passed else if set as enabled it could override this option with `--push`
      platforms: linux/amd64        # Set the platform where Docker images will run, for instance Linux and AMD64. Other supported types can also use 'linux/{architecture}'. Defaults to all available architectures on Ubuntu 20 or later machines running this GitHub runner   If we are pushing our image into GHCR server then if set as enabled it could be passed else false
      registry: ghcr.io           # Set the Docker Registry where images should go, default is 'ghcr.io' for private repositories or use of package repository login Server with `login_server`   If we are pushing our image into GHCR server then if set as enabled it could be passed else false
      tags: true                   # Set to enable the tagging operation on Docker images, defaults off because only one repo can have a single default branch (usually 'main'). For this CI step or jobs using `tags` we'd expect an existing Git Tag with name of image. In such case if set as enabled else disabled
      username: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_USERNAME }} # Username for Docker registry, use the secret DOCKERHUB/PASSWORD (GitHub user password) in `dockerhub-password` – only required when using GHR package repository Login Server with 'username'
      password: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_PASSWORD }} # Password for Docker registry, use the secret DOCKERHUB/PASSWORD (GitHub user password) in `dockerhub-password` – only required when using GHR package repository Login Server with 'username'
